eli3: The Mann-Whitney U test is the non-parametric alternative to the two-sample t-test. Instead of comparing means, it asks: if you pick one observation from group 1 and one from group 2 at random, how often is the group-1 value larger? This probability is P(X‚āĀ > X‚āā) ‚ÄĒ the test checks whether this is 0.5 (no difference) or not.

The test works by combining both samples, ranking all values from 1 to n‚āĀ+n‚āā, then computing the sum of ranks in each group. If both groups have the same distribution, the rank sums should be approximately equal. If one group has consistently higher values, its rank sum will be larger.

Because it uses ranks rather than raw values, Mann-Whitney makes no normality assumption. It is robust to outliers, works with ordinal data, and is valid for any continuous distribution. The cost is slightly lower power than the t-test when the normality assumption actually holds.

whenWhy.title: Non-normal data or small samples
whenWhy.body: When the normality assumption is untenable (severely skewed data, ordinal outcomes, small n), Mann-Whitney is the appropriate alternative to the two-sample t-test. It is exact for any distribution and any n ‚ÄĒ no large-sample approximation needed (though normal approximation works well for n‚āĀ,n‚āā ‚Č• 10).

whenWhy.title: Ordinal outcomes
whenWhy.body: Pain scales (0‚Äď10), Likert scales (1‚Äď5), severity ratings, and education levels are ordinal ‚ÄĒ the intervals between ranks are not equal. Computing means of ordinal data is questionable; comparing rank distributions is not. Mann-Whitney is the standard test for ordinal outcomes in medical and social research.

whenWhy.title: Comparing medians or stochastic dominance
whenWhy.body: Mann-Whitney tests the null H‚āÄ: P(X‚āĀ > X‚āā) = 0.5, which is equivalent to testing equal distributions (not just equal medians ‚ÄĒ a common misconception). The test statistic U/n‚āĀn‚āā estimates P(X‚āĀ > X‚āā), which is the AUC of a ROC curve comparing the two distributions.

whenWhy.title: Robust to outliers
whenWhy.body: Because ranks are used, extreme outliers move at most one rank position (from being the largest to being even larger). The t-test statistic is heavily affected by one extreme outlier inflating s; Mann-Whitney's U statistic is not. This makes it preferable for data with heavy tails or suspected measurement errors.

deepDiveIntro: Given two independent samples X‚āĀ,‚Ä¶,X‚āô‚āĀ and Y‚āĀ,‚Ä¶,Y‚āô‚āā, compute U‚āĀ = ő£ŠĶĘ ő£‚Īľ 1{XŠĶĘ > Y‚Īľ} (number of times a group-1 observation exceeds a group-2 observation). Note U‚āĀ + U‚āā = n‚āĀn‚āā. Test statistic: U = min(U‚āĀ,U‚āā). Under H‚āÄ (same distribution), E[U‚āĀ] = n‚āĀn‚āā/2. Equivalently, compute the Wilcoxon rank-sum statistic W = sum of ranks in group 1; U‚āĀ = W ‚ąí n‚āĀ(n‚āĀ+1)/2.

For large samples (n‚āĀ,n‚āā ‚Č• 10), U is approximately normal: z = (U‚āĀ ‚ąí n‚āĀn‚āā/2) / ‚ąö(n‚āĀn‚āā(n‚āĀ+n‚āā+1)/12) under H‚āÄ. For ties, apply the continuity correction to the variance. For small samples, exact critical values are tabulated.

deepDive.heading: Test Statistic U
deepDive.body: U‚āĀ = #{(i,j): XŠĶĘ > Y‚Īľ} counts concordant pairs. U‚āā = #{(i,j): Y‚Īľ > XŠĶĘ}. U‚āĀ + U‚āā = n‚āĀn‚āā (ties excluded). Rank-sum connection: rank both groups together (1 to n‚āĀ+n‚āā). W‚āĀ = sum of ranks of group 1. U‚āĀ = W‚āĀ ‚ąí n‚āĀ(n‚āĀ+1)/2. Under H‚āÄ: E[U‚āĀ] = n‚āĀn‚āā/2; Var[U‚āĀ] = n‚āĀn‚āā(n‚āĀ+n‚āā+1)/12 (no ties). [1]

deepDive.heading: Large-Sample Normal Approximation
deepDive.body: For n‚āĀ,n‚āā ‚Č• 10: z = (U‚āĀ ‚ąí n‚āĀn‚āā/2) / ‚ąö(n‚āĀn‚āā(n‚āĀ+n‚āā+1)/12) ~ N(0,1) under H‚āÄ. With ties: variance correction needed ‚ÄĒ Var[U‚āĀ] = [n‚āĀn‚āā/(n(n‚ąí1))][n¬≥‚ąín‚ąíő£t‚Īľ(t‚Īľ¬≤‚ąí1)]/12 where t‚Īľ is the size of each tied group. Reject H‚āÄ if |z| > z_{őĪ/2}. [1]

deepDive.heading: U as AUC and Effect Size
deepDive.body: U‚āĀ/(n‚āĀn‚āā) estimates P(X > Y) ‚ÄĒ the probability that a randomly chosen X exceeds a randomly chosen Y. This is exactly the AUC of the ROC curve for classifying group membership using the continuous variable. Under H‚āÄ: AUC = 0.5. Effect size measure: r = z/‚ąön (small: 0.1, medium: 0.3, large: 0.5 by Cohen's convention). [1][2]

deepDive.heading: Assumptions and What the Test Actually Tests
deepDive.body: Mann-Whitney tests H‚āÄ: F‚āĀ = F‚āā (equal distributions), not just equal medians. If distributions have different shapes but same median, H‚āÄ may still be rejected. It assumes: (1) independent random samples; (2) ordinal or continuous outcome; (3) same shape under H‚āÄ (if testing for a location shift only). It does NOT assume normality, equal variances, or equal sample sizes. [1]

takeaways: U‚āĀ = #{pairs where X > Y}; under H‚āÄ: E[U‚āĀ] = n‚āĀn‚āā/2; equivalent to Wilcoxon rank-sum test. [1]
U‚āĀ/(n‚āĀn‚āā) = PŐā(X>Y) = AUC ‚ÄĒ links non-parametric testing directly to classifier evaluation. [1][2]
Large-sample approximation: z = (U‚āĀ‚ąín‚āĀn‚āā/2)/‚ąö(n‚āĀn‚āā(n‚āĀ+n‚āā+1)/12) ~ N(0,1) for n‚āĀ,n‚āā ‚Č• 10. [1]
No normality assumption; valid for ordinal data and any continuous distribution. [1]
Lower power than t-test when normality holds (ARE = 3/ŌÄ ‚Čą 0.955 for normal data). [1]
Tests H‚āÄ: F‚āĀ=F‚āā, not H‚āÄ: median‚āĀ=median‚āā ‚ÄĒ a common misconception. [1]

edgeCases.q: What is the ARE (asymptotic relative efficiency) of Mann-Whitney vs t-test?
edgeCases.a: For normally distributed data: ARE = 3/ŌÄ ‚Čą 0.955. This means Mann-Whitney needs about 1/0.955 ‚Čą 1.05√ó as many observations as the t-test to achieve the same power ‚ÄĒ almost identical efficiency. For heavier-tailed distributions (e.g. Laplace, t with few df), the ARE exceeds 1 ‚ÄĒ Mann-Whitney is MORE efficient than the t-test. This makes it a nearly free robustness improvement. [1]

edgeCases.q: How do ties affect the Mann-Whitney test?
edgeCases.a: Ties between observations in the same group don't matter (they don't affect between-group comparisons). Ties between groups are assigned mid-ranks (average of tied positions). The variance formula must be corrected for ties. Many implementations handle this automatically. If >25% of observations are tied, consider the sign test or a permutation test instead. [1]

sources.label: Casella & Berger ‚ÄĒ Statistical Inference (2nd ed.), Ch. 10
